title: How a specimen earns its grade
excerpt: The things that decide a piece's quality, in plain terms, from a collector's checklist.
body: Grading a mineral is less about a single number and more about reading a piece the way a collector would. We start with the crystal itself. Is the luster bright or dull, is the color even and true to the species, are the crystal faces sharp or worn. A vivid, well formed crystal with strong luster sits at the top, and a cloudy or etched one sits lower even if it is large.

Next we look at condition. We check the display faces for chips, contacts where the specimen grew against something else, and any repairs or restoration. A repair does not ruin a specimen, but it changes what it is worth, so it has to be disclosed.

Then we weigh aesthetics. How the crystals are arranged, how the specimen sits, whether it has a clean natural base all factor in. Finally we consider rarity for the locality, since a common mineral from a famous find can still be special. None of this is a formula. It is judgment, applied the same way every time, and written down so you can check our reasoning against the piece in your hands.

title: What a locality tells you
excerpt: Why the mine a piece came from is part of the science, and part of the price.
body: When we say a specimen is from Mibladen, Morocco or Novo Horizonte, Brazil, we are not just listing a place. A locality is a fingerprint. Certain mines produce a certain look, a habit and color and crystal style that experienced collectors can recognize on sight.

Locality tells you what to expect and what to check. Vanadinite from Mibladen tends toward bright red hexagonal crystals. Cobaltoan calcite from the Congo carries that unmistakable pink. Knowing the source helps you judge whether a piece is typical, exceptional, or possibly mislabeled. Vague labels like "Brazil" or "Africa" are a signal to ask more questions.

We list locality as specifically as the specimen allows. When the exact mine is known, we name it. When it is not, we tell you that too, rather than inventing detail to make a piece sound better than its paperwork supports.

title: What fades and what dissolves
excerpt: Which pieces fade in the sun, which dissolve in water, and how to keep a specimen at its best.
body: Minerals feel permanent, but some are surprisingly fragile once they leave the ground. Light is the first thing to watch. Certain species fade with long sun exposure, including amethyst, celestite, and some fluorite, whose color can weaken over years on a bright windowsill. Keep light sensitive pieces out of direct sun.

Water and humidity are the next concern. A few minerals are water soluble or absorb moisture from the air, halite and some sulfates among them, and can slowly degrade in a damp room. Pyrite can react with humidity over time and break down. Stable, moderate humidity protects most collections.

Dust and handling do quiet damage too. Fingertips leave oils, and repeated wiping can scratch soft minerals or dull a delicate luster. Use a soft brush or gentle air rather than a cloth on fragile crystals. Fossils and shells like the same conditions. Treat a specimen as the record it is, and it will outlast all of us.

title: The Mohs scale in plain terms
excerpt: What mineral hardness actually measures, how to test it with things in your pocket, and why it changes how you handle a piece.
body: Hardness on the Mohs scale measures one specific thing: whether one mineral can scratch another. It runs 1 to 10, from talc soft enough to mark with a fingernail up to diamond, the hardest natural material there is. It is a ranking, not a math scale, so the jump from 9 to 10 is far bigger than the jump from 2 to 3.

You can place most minerals with things you already carry. A fingernail is about 2.5, so if it leaves a mark you are in halite or gypsum territory. A copper coin sits near 3.5. A steel knife blade or nail is about 5.5, and window glass is close behind. Quartz, at 7, will scratch all of those and is the line between soft display minerals and durable ones.

Hardness is not just trivia, it tells you how to live with a piece. A soft fluorite at 4 or a bladed halite at 2 needs a gentle shelf, no wiping with grit, and no knocking against harder specimens that will scar it. A quartz or pyrite shrugs off handling. When we list a piece we show where it falls on the scale for exactly this reason, so you know before it arrives whether it wants a glass case or can sit out on a desk.

One honest caution: never test a finished specimen by scratching its display face. Hardness testing leaves a permanent mark. Collectors test on a hidden spot or a broken edge, or they trust the published hardness for the species, which is what the number on our pages is for.

title: What makes a fossil worth keeping
excerpt: Completeness, preservation, and honest prep matter more than size. How to read a fossil the way a collector does.
body: With fossils, the first thing to read is completeness. A whole fish with its head, tail, fins, and spine intact is worth far more than a bigger slab with pieces missing, and a full tooth beats a chipped one every time. Nature rarely preserves things perfectly, so the complete pieces are the ones that command a price.

Preservation is next. It covers how sharp the detail is, how good the contrast is between the fossil and the rock around it, and whether the surface is crisp or worn. A Green River fish that stands out dark against pale limestone reads better than one you have to squint at, even if both are the same species and age.

Then comes prep and honesty. Many fossils are found in fragments and glued back together, and a clean repair is completely normal. What matters is that it is disclosed. Restoration, where material is actually added that was not originally there, is a bigger deal and has to be called out every time. We tell you when a piece has been repaired or restored, and roughly how much, because that is the difference between a fair price and a bad surprise.

Last is context, the age and the formation. Knowing a fish came from the Green River Formation of the Eocene, or a tooth from the Moroccan phosphate beds, is part of what you are buying. It places the piece in deep time and lets you tell its story. Where we know the age and origin, we put it right on the page. Where we do not, we say so rather than invent one.
